[29:30] I want you to reflect. Go back to these three habits and see whether or not you were in congruency with these three habits. Most cases, nine out of ten times, when a deal doesn't go through, it's because you were out of congruency with these three habits.

[31:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Habit #1: Don't Make Excuses - Extreme Ownership"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[31:25] And then all of a sudden, they change their mind. It's not a fair world. It doesn't matter. We take extreme ownership. We don't make excuses.

 

[31:45] Marketing sent me bad leads. It's not my fault. The customers are hard to deal with. Right? Wah, wah, wah, wah, wah. So we don't make excuses. Can we agree on that? We don't make excuses.

[32:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "SDR Definition - Sales Development Rep"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Definição e explicação de SDR (Sales Development Rep) vs BDR (Business Development Rep) vs Appointment Setters, hierarquia de sofisticação

 

[32:30] Those of you in the B2C or the B2B team, don't type in the chat. Sales Development Rep. That's correct. Now, if you don't know this word, don't be embarrassed. Let us know as well, too.

 

[32:50] Have you ever encountered this term, SDR? If you didn't, then pay attention and just let us know. We will help you. It's good, SDRs. This is a very common industry standard term.

 

[33:10] And you might see other variations of it, BDR, SDR. BDR stands for Business Development Rep. SDR stands for Sales Development Rep. Sometimes there's other terms like Appointment Setters.

 

[33:35] But Appointment Setters have a connotation of being a little bit more of a lower level. SDRs are more sophisticated. SDRs communicate with C-suite executives, director levels, manager levels.

 

[34:00] Appointment Setters, they're just kind of like a dime a dozen. They're just kind of nonstop ringing on the phones. SDRs are more professional. They ask very strategic questions. They have to get through the gatekeepers.

 

[34:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Importance of SDRs in B2B"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Destaque do papel crítico dos SDRs: criam receita previsível, mantêm fluxo consistente de appointments para AEs, essenciais durante recessão

 

[34:30] So they're very, very important. In fact, if anything at all, if you speak to most VP of sales, chief revenue officers, and you ask them, who are some of the key members on your team? They will always say the SDRs.

 

[35:00] Because the SDRs will have to lead the charge. They are the ones that actually set the appointments. That's how they create a predictable revenue. They are the ones when recession is up or down, you're not depending on inbound leads.

 

[35:25] They're the ones that keep the consistent flow of appointments coming in for the AEs, the account executives. Now, who knows what the term AE stands for?

 

[35:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "AE Definition - Account Executive"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Definição de AE (Account Executive) como o "closer" no mundo B2B, equivalente profissional do closer em HTC

 

[35:50] Account executive. What does that mean? AE? Yep. AE closes a sale. That's correct. Account executive. So account executive, I want you to think of it.

 

[36:10] If you're already a closer, as a closer, you just refer to yourself as a sales professional, as a closer. Well, in the professional world, the B2B world, a closer is actually known as an AE.

 

[36:35] Now, there are some other variations to this. But in general, if you were to speak to most corporations, SMBs, SMB stands for small, mid-sized businesses, small businesses, right? Small to medium-sized businesses.

 

[37:00] SMEs, SMBs. Many of them, or even the bigger enterprise companies, if you mention to them an AE, they understand. An AE is a rainmaker. AE partnered up with an SDR. Those two are a tag team.

 

[37:25] Very critical and crucial for our business success and revenue. Okay? So they are very in demand, SDRs and AEs. Moving on.

 

[37:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Habit #2: Build Credibility"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pergunta central sobre o significado de credibilidade, preparação para definição da comunidade

 

[37:50] Number two. Write this down. Ultra-high ticket closers build credibility. Now, don't go Google this right now. What does credibility mean to you? Don't Google it. Just type in the chat. What does credibility mean?

 

[38:15] Trust. Dana, maybe help me out. I'm seeing trust. Trustworthy. A lot of trusts. Honesty, reputation. Ownership. Validation. Transparency. Respect. Integrity. Consistency. Knowledge. Good. Proven result.

 

[38:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Credibility Matrix - Integrity vs Capability"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente

 

VISUAL: Matriz 2x2 com dois eixos: vertical = Integrity (trustworthy, reliability, consistency), horizontal = Capability (problem solving, delivery). Zona central = "Credibility Value Zone"

 

[38:50] So take a look at this. Take a screenshot of this. This is another way to measure B2B sales. Very different from B2C. B2C is very transactional. You're dealing many times in mass quantities.

 

[39:15] B2B, a lot of times, this is unseen. It's invisible, but it exists. And you want to have on the two axes, on the left axis, the one that goes vertical is integrity. On the horizontal axis, capability.

 

[39:40] Capability to solve problems. Capability to deliver. So on the vertical axis, there's trustworthy, which we saw many of you, right? Reliability. Being consistent. And there's many, many more adjectives to describe what constitutes the integrity side.

 

[40:10] But then there's also the capability. Can your company, can the product or service you're representing, can your business bring the capabilities, which translates into the minds of the business owner or the C-suite executives, are you going to be that extra pair of hands?

 

[40:40] Are you the expert consultant? Can you take some bandwidth away from them? Perhaps they don't have the bandwidth to deal with those issues and you're the specialist, you're the expert.

 

[41:05] Now, in combining these two together, the vertical and the horizontal axes, you get into a zone which is called the credibility value zone.

 

[41:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Credibility Value Zone - Application"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Aplicação prática da matriz de credibilidade: como usar para diagnóstico quando vendas não fecham, importância de manter ambos os eixos altos

 

[41:30] So once again, going back to, let's just future pace a bit. Let's go into the future. It's been a week. It's been a month. It's been a couple of months. And a sale did not go through.

 

[42:00] Look back at this image because this is one of the habits about credibility. Did you build that credibility? Did you position, was your company positioned at that trusted advisor to become that collaborative relationship?

 

[42:30] Was your vernacular in the way you communicated to your client, did they feel through your questions that you were there and you actually cared about that business as if you were a stakeholder in their company, that extra pair of hands, that you are reliable, everything.

 

[43:00] How you do anything is how you do everything. Could you be seen as that? And if not, if at any point it faltered, in the B2B world, sometimes you're dealing with more than one decision maker, stakeholder.

 

[43:30] If you're not communicating this through your actions, your professionalism, your business acumen, if you're not communicating that to all the stakeholders, that may be that one card that brings down your whole house of cards and that's why the deal did not go through.

 

[44:00] So this, it's a higher stakes game. Let's just face it folks, it's a higher stakes game. Or you can go back to selling TVs and selling cars and selling jewelry, whatever it is in the B2C world.

 

[44:25] Again, nothing wrong with that. Many of our high ticket closers have done very well in real estate. They've done very well in some of the higher ticket of the high ticket products. And they make a great six-figure income.

 

[44:50] But the B2B world, that's a whole different ballgame. Okay. Are you getting value from this? Is this starting to make sense? Yes? Okay.

 

[45:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Habit #3: Be The Trusted Advisor - Sun Tzu"

 

NÚMEROS: 506 BC (data histórica)

 

VISUAL: História de Sun Tzu e King Helu, conceito de trusted strategic advisor, referência ao livro "The Art of War", aplicação em B2B moderno

 

[45:15] Take a screenshot of this. This is important. Print this out. Put it on your wall. As you are on the phone speaking with a CEO or a CFO, another business owner. Maybe you are a business owner that sells to smaller business owners as well.

 

[45:45] Ask yourself, where do you fit at any given time on a call, during the emails, the communications? Ask yourself where you stand.

 

[46:05] Ultra high ticket closers, number three, ultra high ticket closers are the trusted advisors, which you can see how we're stacking on top of two. We are the trusted advisors.

 

[46:30] So many of you have heard of Dan talk about Sun Tzu, which is the art of war. This is very critical. It's very different from B2C sales in that B2B sales, we are that advisor.

 

[46:55] So as you can see here, it's a little bit of backstory for those of you who are not familiar with the Sun Tzu story, right? It is in 506 BC. A Chinese emperor by the name of King Helu launched multiple successful attacks, which led to the victories and securities for his state.

 

[47:25] Now his success was largely attributed to his trusted strategic advisor, Sun Tzu, who helped the emperor build the dynasty. It wasn't just the emperor himself. The emperor relied on that trusted advisor.

 

[47:55] So Sun Tzu's most famous work, The Art of War, which is used in companies all around the world. Management will usually have junior managers or middle managers read the book, and you would be very wise to also pick up the book if you've never read it.

 conductbo

[48:25] There are many English versions these days. Read the book, understand it, and apply the philosophies behind it. It is still relevant in this day and age in the way we conduct business. Be the trusted advisor for your clients.

[57:35] Let's talk about the next step here. Many times in B2B, they will offer you a solid base and a larger variable. So the variable is actually the commission, right? In layman's terms, layperson's terms, OT means, we saw it before, type it again in the chat, what does OTE stand for?

 

[58:05] On target earnings, that's correct. In the B2B world, they don't call it commissions, they call it on target earnings. Why? Because in the B2B world, you are normally held accountable to KPIs, OKRs, right?

[59:35] And relationships measured in years, you know, the lifetime value of a B2B transaction could sometimes be six, seven figures that you could actually have in one transaction.

 

[1:00:05] Just like you saw in Danny Lin's deals, the relationship, the time it took her to nurture those to close that deal, it didn't happen in one call close, it didn't happen, you know, just probably in one or two days, though they do happen sometimes.

 

[1:00:35] In general, most B2B transactions tend to be a little bit longer. They tend to be one week, three weeks, one month, sometimes even six months or even a year. I've seen deals take that much time to close. So that's the B2B world.

[1:13:05] So you can even see the senior AE. I mean, not bad. It's about a $220,000 a year annual OT. Who agrees that $220,000 is not bad? Right? And you probably even have your weekends off if you want to have your weekends off.

 

[1:13:35] So you're not a business owner. You're not working seven days a week. As a senior AE, you come in. You work with a team, $220,000. Now, that's just an average. There's obviously a spectrum on both sides. It goes up and down. Okay?

 

[1:14:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Business Owners - B2B Deal Structuring"

[1:17:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Digital Marketing Agency Deal Structure Example"

 

NÚMEROS: $10K/mês retainer, 10% rev share, $120K/ano (retainer), $500K (10% de $5M crescimento) = $620K total

 

VISUAL: Breakdown detalhado de estrutura de deal: serviços (funnels, copy, ClickFunnels, Infusionsoft, Active Campaign, Facebook Ads, Google Ads) + compensação (retainer + rev share)

 

[1:17:15] So let's give an example here. Okay. Let's say, for example, and this is pretty normal, you're charging $10,000 per month as a retainer for your services. You're going to build out all their funnels. You're going to create their campaigns, help them write the copy.

 

[1:17:45] You're going to help create it, whether it's on ClickFunnels or Infusionsoft, have the active campaign set up. You're going to do all those things. You're going to be in their Facebook ads manager, maybe their Google, and you're going to help them purchase their ads, and you're going to maintain it.

 

[1:18:15] And you structure the deal where you're being paid $10,000 per month, plus you take a 10% rev share. So very simple math. You're already earning about $120,000 per year, plus $500,000.

 

[1:18:45] Do you see how that 10% rev share, that $500,000, where did that come from? That's $5 million on the uptake, right? So you have $5 million that they were already at. You help them bring up another $5 million, and you took 10% of that.

 

[1:19:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "UHTC as Agency Closer - Alternative Structure"

 

NÚMEROS: $2K/mês (do retainer de $10K), 2% rev share (de $5M = $100K), total possível como closer

 

VISUAL: Estrutura alternativa para closers trabalhando com business owners: porcentagem menor do retainer + porcentagem menor do rev share

 

[1:19:20] Do you see how structuring a B2B deal like this can be such much larger numbers? The digits are much bigger. Instead of doing a $300 deal or a $50 deal, structuring a deal like this can be very helpful.

 

[1:19:50] Now, if you're not the business owner, maybe you are just an ultra-high-ticket closer that's helping a business owner. Maybe you negotiate with the business owner. You take a percentage of that.

 

[1:20:20] Maybe you're not making the $10,000 per month retainer. Maybe the business owner is paying you $2,000 a month out of that $10,000. And maybe you take only 2% of the rev share. Can you do the math? What if you took 2% out of that $5 million?

 

[1:20:50] This all depends on whether you have the skills to negotiate the deals. That's right. Okay, let's give you another example here.

 

[1:21:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "EB-5 Visa Investment Deal Structure"

 

NÚMEROS: 4 deals/mês, $500K/investor, 5% comissão = $100K/mês × 12 = $1.2M/ano; 2 deals/mês = $600K/ano; 1 deal/mês = $300K/ano

 

VISUAL: Múltiplos cenários de EB-5: 4 deals, 2 deals, 1 deal por mês, mostrando escalabilidade e potencial de renda mesmo começando devagar

 

[1:21:20] Let's just talk about the EB-5 visa, for example, right? You broker the EB-5 visa property investment deals that we talked about earlier on, and you do four deals a month. Now, four deals averaging about $500,000 per investor, right?

 

[1:21:50] And you structure it to have a 5% commission, a 5% variable. Okay, so that's $100,000, and you multiply by 12, over 12 months in a year. That's $1.2 million.

 

[1:22:20] But maybe most of you can't even figure out how to do that. Maybe that's too big for you. What if you only did half of that? What if you only did two a month? Because you're just getting started, and you structured these deals.

 

[1:22:50] You did two per month at $500,000 per investor times 5%. That's $50,000 times 12. That's $600,000. I mean, what if you only did one a month? That's still $300,000.

 

[1:23:20] Can you see yourself doing that? Is that something that you might be interested to learn how to do? This is why we asked the question, do you think maybe you were selling to the wrong audience?

 

[1:23:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2C vs B2B Comparison - Wrong Audience Concept"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Comparação filosófica: não parar B2C, mas adicionar B2B como skill stack, conceito de "selling to wrong audience" como limitador

 

[1:23:55] This is the difference between just B2C versus B2B. And once again, we're not telling you not to stop doing B2C. Keep mastering your B2C, but in the meantime, start to learn the skill of B2B.

 

[1:24:25] And for those of you who are already in B2B, use these classes to level up. Use these classes to close more deals of bigger value, smoother, have longer lifetime value for each of the transactions. Does that make sense?

[1:57:20] B2B needs more specialized skills in specific areas of the sales process. That's it. So as long as you can plug into a system, if you can learn that, and we will give you as much as we can, real practical, what we've used, what other companies, our clients are using, and we're talking clients that are SMBs and enterprise companies are using.

 

[1:57:55] Would you like that? If you could use that, do you need to go and do B2C sales first? Maybe you're doing B2C, that's great, keep doing it. But if you've never done it, and you want to start off fresh, you can actually start with B2B.

[2:03:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "UHTC Secret #1: Inside vs Outside Sales"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[2:03:30] So let's get into it. Ultra high ticket closing secret number one. Sometimes it requires face-to-face to seal the deal. Inside B2B sales versus outside B2B sales.

 

[2:04:00] This is a little bit more advanced. We might cover it a little bit more as we go through the weeks here. For now, most of you will be thinking of B2B sales as what we call inside sales.

 

[2:04:30] Who knows the difference between inside sales versus outside sales? We'll cover this a little bit later on, but just type in the chat. Who knows the difference between inside sales versus outside sales?

 

[2:05:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Inside vs Outside Sales Definitions"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Definições iniciais: Outside sales = field sales, meeting clients, attending events; Inside sales = phone, email, mas não limitado a isso

 

[2:05:05] Now, naturally with the way things are going on with social distancing and everything happening around the world, there's not as much of the outside sales going on right now. But things are not gonna remain this way forever.

 

[2:05:35] And so yes, outside sales, field sales, meeting clients, attending events. Yep, where you go out and you present. So the secret is you don't need to. This is the relying only on phone and email.

 

[2:06:05] In B2C or even in HTC, we taught you to do a lot of closing by phone. But we also touched a bit on attending events. That is a form of outside sales.

 

[2:06:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Inside Sales Components"

 

NÚMEROS: 80-90% (porcentagem do deal feito remotamente antes de face-to-face)

 

VISUAL: Descrição de inside sales: marketing team, marketing meetings, SDRs, phone calls, trabalho de office/call center/remoto/home, business owners comunicando

 

[2:06:40] And then with B2B internal sales, many times you've got a marketing team, you've got marketing meetings, you've got SDRs, you're taking phone calls, whether you work in an office, in a call center, whether you work remotely from home, whether you're a business owner, whether you communicate to other business owners.

 

[2:07:15] Many times we think we only communicate through emails or phone. Not true. Many times some of the biggest deals are done face-to-face to seal the deal.

 

[2:07:45] Maybe the initial stages, you do 80%, 90% of the deal is done over the phones, the Zoom. But to seal the deal, maybe you're meeting at that hotel. Maybe you're flying to meet them at their office. To seal the deal.

 

[2:08:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Danny Lin EB-5 Example - Face-to-Face Closing"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados (menção humorística de "bag of cash")

 

VISUAL: Exemplo prático: Danny Lin fez maioria por phone/Zoom, mas sealed deals face-to-face, às vezes com cliente voando para encontro presencial

 

[2:08:20] Danny Lin was closing some of those EB-5 deals. Most of it done by phone, Zoom. And then after that, maybe seal the deal. Client flies over with a bag of cash. Literally in one of them. Just teasing Danny there.

 

[2:08:50] But that wasn't a deal that happened with EB-5. But literally seal the deal might need to be done face-to-face. So don't limit yourself to thinking only that emails or phones.

 

[2:09:20] Yes, a lot of our work is done. The correct type of emails, the correct kind of phone calls are very necessary for B2B. But also consider that some of the biggest deals are done with outside sales.

 

[2:09:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Inside vs Outside Sales - Detailed Comparison"

 

NÚMEROS: 35,000+ (deals típicos em outside sales), 6-figure to 7-figure income (inside sales potential)

 

VISUAL: Tabela comparativa completa: Inside (prospecting, cold calls/emails, office work, smaller recurring deals, lower costs, sales tools) vs Outside (relationship building, networking, travel, larger deals $35K+, high close rate, longer sales cycle)

 

[2:09:55] So let me share with you a bit of the difference between inside and outside sales. Inside sales, prospecting clients, right? You're doing a lot of prospecting. There's SDRs. There's also depending on inbound.

 

[2:10:25] There's cold emails, cold calls, lots of clients working from an office, smaller recurring deals, using sales tools, LinkedIn, social networks, lower costs, right? Not too bad. Six-figure income, even maybe a seven-figure income doing this.

 

[2:10:55] Outside sales, you build relationships, networking. You do this during events, masterminds. Just a couple of, you just have a couple of clients you're working most of the time.

 

[2:11:25] Traveling to meet the clients when you're selling enterprise to maybe Oracle or SAP or maybe Cisco. Many times this may require you to go on a business trip to travel.

 

[2:11:55] Usually deals larger than 35,000 relying on word of mouth, persuasion, high close rate. When you have the trust, when you're that trusted advisor, it may be a longer sales cycle.

 

[2:12:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Outside Sales Requirements & Community Response"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente

 

VISUAL: Requirements de outside sales: operating capital para travel, business trips, airplane, reuniões presenciais; pedido para comunidade compartilhar quem faz outside sales

 

[2:12:30] You may need to have that operating capital to travel, to jump on an airplane, to go on a business trip, close that deal. How many of you, actually, just out of curiosity, type in the chat, how many of you are business owners that have to do outside sales for your business?

 

[2:13:00] He says, him, yes, I know Keith because he travels to China to meet with the buyers or with Japan. Okay, John does it, yes, John definitely does a lot of B2B. Resmic sometimes. Carolyn says yes.

 

[2:13:30] Anthony, when he had his jewelry business, absolutely. Okay, and also Joseph, JJ, yep, with his video productions company sometimes. Okay, Mustafa, body language in outside sales.

 

[2:14:00] That might be something, it's interesting. We may or may not cover that. We've got some videos that have covered in the past, but no promises. Okay, Mustafa, we'll try to cover it, but no promises. Maybe if we've got some bonus time, we'll jump into that.

 

[2:14:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Common B2B Mistake #2: Chasing Only Enterprise Deals"

VISUAL: Erro comum destacado: pular direto para enterprise sales sem experiência, Dan Lok também explorou enterprise mas recomenda começar com SMB

 

[2:14:35] So common B2B mistake number two, chasing only enterprise deals. Some of you don't even have the experience yet, and you're trying to jump into B2B. So you get this feeling that you have to do enterprise sales.

 

[2:15:05] Now, how many of you actually from some of the earlier seasons, maybe you've been with us since, anyone before season four or five? Kenny's been season one. Chris is season one. Jake, Buckley, Jazz, Yao, Joanne, Heidi, right?

 

[2:15:35] So you know that at one point, Dan was also exploring in the enterprise sales side of things. And even that too, we were explaining to the closers, you don't need to jump into enterprise sales right away.

 

[2:16:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "SMB Definition & Sweet Spot"

VISUAL: Definição de SMB (Small to Medium-sized Business): tamanho típico, revenue range, sweet spot para começar em B2B

 

[2:16:10] What you wanna do is start off with SMB. Start focusing on the smaller companies. Company, what is SMB? Who knows what, on average, what is an SMB? How big is their revenue? How many employees do they have? What is the average?

 

[2:16:40] Dan says under 200. Delia says 50. Small to medium-sized businesses, yeah. Up to about 50 million in revenue. Usually sweet spot around about 10 million, 5, 10 million.

[2:17:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Critical Questions for Business Owners - Screenshot This!"

[2:18:25] We're getting into the really serious parts. Aside from the front, which is very important about the mindset, this is very important. Ask yourself when you communicate to another business owner, it doesn't matter if they're a plumber, a dentist, an owner of an insurance brokerage, of a real estate brokerage, a business owner that owns a SaaS, how is it Desmond is able to communicate?

 

[2:19:00] You ask yourself, replace my name with your name. And how is it I've been able over the years to be able to communicate, travel? Why does Dan send me on business trips?

 

[2:19:30] Because he knows that when I communicate to them, and I'm not perfect either, my mentor is always giving me tips on how to get better. It gets harder. It gets even more harder. It gets harder on me even more, the bigger the deals we're dealing with.

 

[2:20:00] But how does he trust me to do that? It's because he knows that when I'm there, I represent, I behave and act as a business owner of the highest caliber.

 

[2:20:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Do They Feel You Understand Their Business? - Core Questions"

 

NÚMEROS: 3 meses (tempo de nurturing mencionado)

 

VISUAL: Perguntas críticas repetidas e expandidas: entende o negócio? fez research? perguntou questões certas? está nutrindo por 3 meses? oferecendo valor sem cobrar?

 

[2:20:35] So let me ask you a question. Do they feel you understand their business, their materials, their budgets, their industry, clients, marketing, their challenges? Let me repeat that. This is important. It's worth spending some time on.

 

[2:21:05] Do they feel you understand their business? Or are you there to sell them something? Do they feel you understand their business? Did you do your research? Did you ask the right questions?

 

[2:21:35] Are you trying to close them in one call or maybe nurture the relationship over three months, keep giving value, keep offering them, asking them questions, finding out what some pains are, and then just finding a solution for them without charging them anything?

 

[2:22:05] Do the clients understand? There's a lot of work that you can do before the call, then you ask during the call, you keep your mouth shut, you just ask questions, you write lots of notes, and then you go research and you get answers for them.

 

[2:22:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Become the Trusted Advisor - Bandwidth Relief"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Conexão com credibility matrix anterior: demonstrar que entende challenges e marketing, tirar bandwidth deles, ser trusted advisor

 

[2:22:40] You become that trusted advisor. Do you remember that image I showed earlier on? Where you can take some of the bandwidth away from them, help relieve? That's when you demonstrate you understand their challenges and their marketing.

 

[2:23:10] So do you understand what is important to them? Really, do you really understand what's important to them? What pains they're facing, what they cannot discuss with their employees or staff?

 

[2:23:40] Do they see you as a salesperson or as a fellow business owner and a partner? Perhaps someone who has experienced services or tools that can help them solve their business challenges as well as get them to their goals.

 

[2:24:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Michael's Example - Perceived vs Actual Business Owner Status"

[2:24:45] But I'm using him because we work in the B2B team and Michael was saying to me that he goes, Desmond, I'm not a business owner unlike you. And he had sure had me fooled because the way Michael speaks, the way he prepares documentation, everything else is very professional.

 

[2:25:15] I would have thought Michael has already been in business for several years. So can you see how my perception of him was I thought he was a business owner and until he opened his mouth and told me he was not a business owner, that all came from his own self-limiting belief, right?

[2:26:20] So for example, we have the B2B sales team and the B2B sales team is handling the intake interviews of the Dragon 100. So there's a lot of business owners. This is a real case situation here, right?

 

[2:26:50] Real business owners, quite intimidating, some of them, in fact, right? Some of them, you know, $20 million business, $50 million business yet I don't pretend to be, you know, I may not try to stand toe to toe with, I'm a business owner, so I have that belief that I'm a business owner, but then maybe I'm connecting that business owner to Dan and Dan has the entire advisory board.

 

[2:27:25] And so combined with all the advisory board, the billionaires that are part of the advisory board, the legal, the financial, the organizational, the operational, the IT, the tech, all those individuals, I represent them.

[2:28:30] That's the mindset. That's the presence that I put out to the business owner. Really go back and listen to this part here. This is critical. It is absolutely critical to close B2B sales.

 

[2:29:00] If you can convey that feeling, if you can convey that trust, that integrity, that credibility, you will get the sale.

[2:31:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Executives/Directors - Key Considerations (Screenshot This!)"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Lista crítica para trabalhar com executives: make them feel less risk averse, ability to marshal resources, understand business goals, responsiveness, accountability, problem solving, work well with staff, track record, industry knowledge, understand personal issues

 

[2:31:15] So take a screenshot of this, make a note, block out the time, go back and listen to the replay. So executives or directors, do you know how to make them feel less risk averse?

 

[2:31:45] In the corporate world, many times they're in a position, they're not looking to rock the boat. They may already have a vendor that's providing them service. They've never worked with you before. It's a lot of extra work for them to have to change vendors.

 

[2:32:15] And maybe many times you're dealing with an executive or director, they tend to be middle age or higher. They don't wanna lose that position. Maybe they're waiting for a promotion.

 

[2:32:45] Maybe they just wanna keep their head down, hit their KPIs, their OKRs, and then maybe rotate out of the company, get recruited to another company and take a higher package.

 

[2:33:15] So they don't want to take on a risk of bringing on a new vendor. So you have to be able to communicate that and make them feel less risk averse. We'll cover this more through the questions and through the strategies, the tactics, the philosophies that we'll use.

 

[2:33:45] But I just wanna quickly highlight it here for you, okay? Let's move on. Do you have an ability to marshal resources? This is not my own. Let me just tell you that a lot of this has been studied, has been summarized from some of the...

 

[2:34:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Executive Requirements - Expert Sources"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Disclaimer: informações vêm de experts em B2B sales e enterprise sales, não de livros universitários, conhecimento prático condensado

 

[2:34:20] You won't find this in most of the university business books. This is from the experts. I went around and I asked and I talked to some of the best mentors in B2B sales, enterprise sales. They all summarize, it all narrowed down to these. We shortlisted it down to these.

 

[2:34:50] Do you have the ability to marshal resources? Do you understand their business goals? Are you responsive to their requests? Do you demonstrate your willingness to be held accountable?

 

[2:35:20] Do you demonstrate your ability to solve problems? Do you work well with their staff? Key here. Do you have a track record of accomplishments? Do you have knowledge of their industry?

[2:37:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Stop Being a Salesperson - Green Monster"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[2:37:35] Going back to one of the initial mistakes, many of you behave and act still like salespeople. And if you act like a salesperson, you're in there convincing people in the B2B world.

 

[2:38:05] You're trying to convince, even though you're wearing, same green monster is coming out again. You're still doing the same thing. Still trying to convince.

 

[2:38:35] In B2B, the art of closing is all about asking the right questions, pausing, let them reply, asking the proper follow-up questions.

 

[2:39:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Loneliness at the Top - Executive Paradox"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Conceito de solidão executiva: executives não podem confiar em peers, officers, subordinates; oportunidade para closer se tornar confidente

 

[2:39:10] So it can be very lonely at the top, and this creates a paradox for a lot of the executives or business owners. The very people you might expect an executive to open up to, for example, their peers, officers, subordinates, those are the ones they may actually confide in the least.

 

[2:39:45] Pharmaceutical world, medical, pharmaceutical, in the tech, you might be speaking to a CMO or a CRO of the company, a VP of sales. You might be speaking to that person, and that person might actually say

 

[2:40:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Discovery Questions - Business Understanding Set 1"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Primeira categoria de perguntas organizadas: descoberta do negócio, estrutura para transição através de categorias (business → goals → challenges)

 

[2:40:20] And then internalize it. Understand why you're asking these questions. What are you trying to extract? How are you trying to convey to the business owner or the executive that you are really genuinely concerned about their business?

 

[2:40:50] And then the way to show that you're not just scripting things out and just asking a question for the sake of asking a question is to be able to ask a follow-up question.

 

[2:41:20] When they answer, go deeper. Ask questions why. Is it a nice to have? Is it a need to have? Right? If it's a need to have, why is it a need to have?

 

[2:41:50] On the upside, when things work, good. On the downside, if things don't work, what's the consequences? It's a great opportunity for you to then offer your services at that point.

[2:46:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Role Play - Goals Achievement Questions"

 

NÚMEROS: 2-3 anos (tempo trabalhando no goal), $8.7M (tamanho do negócio de Godfrey)

 

VISUAL: Role play ao vivo demonstrando: "How long working on goal?", "What does better mean?", "What have you tried?", técnicas de follow-up questions

 

[2:46:35] So, well, Godfrey, you know, it's really, it's very insightful. I've written lots of notes here. You've been telling me about some of those nice-to-haves and need-to-haves. Do you mind if I, let me ask you another question.

 

[2:47:05] How long have you been working on getting to that goal? You know, as I said, and the board did give us that recommendation. And we've been, we've tried out a couple of things. We've been doing this for like two years where we've kind of adjusted a couple of things within our company.

 

[2:47:35] And yeah, technically we've been doing this for two, three years and it's been working, but it could be better. Okay. So if I read you correctly, if I'm hearing you correctly, you said it's been working for the last two, three years, but it could be better.

 

[2:48:05] Can you define a bit more about that? What do you mean by better? Give me some specific numbers. What does that mean? You know, 8.7 million is actually a great place to start off with, but there's a lot of work behind it.

 

[2:48:35] Like as already mentioned, the scalability is an important factor. And in the modern times, you know, with technology and all the tools that you have, it's really important for us to optimize it to get the best out of it.

 

[2:49:05] Our target is obviously to increase the revenue, but the profit margin is also a big part of it. The profit margin is a big factor as well too. And what have you tried to achieve your goal?

 

[2:49:35] I mean, you know, what did you have you done to try to overcome the challenges in the past? Oh, where can I start? Basically we did have certain, we did improve our marketing and also our ad system, which helped out a lot, which brought in much traffic.

 

[2:50:05] However, we did have a lot of work, but the quality was not as we wanted it because there's no point in just having a lot of prospects without them being really qualified. You know what I mean?

 

[2:50:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Role Play - Clarifying & Digging Deeper"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Demonstração de técnica: não assumir entendimento, pedir clarificação, fazer follow-up questions mais profundas, descobrir o que funcionou vs o que não funcionou

 

[2:50:40] I do, I understand that. But let me ask you a little bit deeper. I don't want to just assume that I understand the question here. So what you're saying here, what didn't work so well was the ads were not bringing you the right type of targeted leads that you needed. Is that correct?

 

[2:51:10] Yes. And we couldn't catch up with it at the same time. It's kind of a balance. We had a lot of people coming in, but catching up with them or catching up with the right people was what we were trying to optimize in this case.

 

[2:51:40] Okay. And out of all of that, what was actually working well? Was anything working really well at that time? Yes. Our CRM system actually did the work we expected it to do, which is actually coordinated quite well, which was something we implemented last year.

 

[2:52:10] And yeah, it did help us get out of a lot of trouble we could have had if we did not have that system in place. And why do you think that is? Why do you think that CRM was able to help you with that?

 

[2:52:40] Was it a functionality that was just built into the CRM? Was it training, helping the members to use it better? What actually changed? From the feedback of our management, it gave them a good overview of what was happening and where we were having blocking points.

 

[2:53:10] Give us an overview of all the different departments and our sales team. So you were able to leverage the technology to also then give you the proper data, which then allowed the management to make the right decisions, affecting and then circling back to the marketing as well. Is that correct?

 

[2:53:40] Yeah, not only that, but it also helps us know where our weak points are within our structure. Good. So pause the role play. Round of applause for Godfrey. Thank you very much, Godfrey. Okay. Thank you.

 

[2:54:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Role Play Debrief - B2C vs B2B Thinking Differences"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Análise do role play: B2C prospects não pensam tão especificamente quanto B2B, vantagem ao transicionar de B2C para B2B com small business owners

 

[2:54:15] And you'll see here, even Chris Campbell typed, he says, some prospects, you know, sometimes B2C prospects don't even think so specifically. That's correct.

 

[2:54:45] That's the difference between a lot of times B2C world or even sometimes small business owners. And what you'll find, believe it or not, actually, Chris, to your advantage, when you're dealing, especially if you're transitioning from B2C and you're moving to B2B, I would try to look for closing opportunities in an ultra high ticket closing scenario where you're closing B2B, but the smaller business owners. Okay?

 

[2:55:20] And those smaller business owners could be under a million dollars in revenue. Those business owners, when you come in and you leverage and you're asking these sort of questions, now it positions you as an authority.

 

[2:55:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Positioning as Authority with Small Business Owners"

 

NÚMEROS: Sob $1M revenue (small business owners)

 

VISUAL: Estratégia: usar perguntas sofisticadas com small business owners que podem não saber respostas, posicionar como authority sem fazer sentir desconfortável

 

[2:55:55] It's a lot easier to come in as an authority when you're now, sometimes, and I'm very mindful of making them feel uncomfortable. I'm not trying, I'm not there to make them feel uncomfortable when I ask them questions they may not know the answers to.

 

[2:56:25] So I will let them know in a, you know, again, it's in a very nurturing way. I will ask them and I say, look, you know, if you don't know your numbers right now, that's okay. I understand.

 

[2:56:55] It's the whole feel felt found. I understand. I felt the same way, you know. Not all the time. I'm running my business. I'm so busy. I'm taking care of getting the guys out on the trucks. I'm running the, you know, I'm getting the suppliers in here. I barely have any time.

 

[2:57:25] That's okay. But maybe do you not, you know, do you think maybe now might be a good time for us to start thinking about these questions? I'm going to ask you some questions. You don't have to answer me.

 

[2:57:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Nurturing Strategy - Not Closing Today"

 

NÚMEROS: 1 semana (follow-up timeframe)

 

VISUAL: Abordagem de nurturing: "I'm not here to close you today, not here to sell anything, just understand your business, write these questions down, let's connect next week"

 

[2:58:00] Like I said, today I'm not here to close you. I'm not here to try to sell you anything. I'm just here to understand your business. You know, maybe write some of these questions down and let's connect next week.

 

[2:58:30] And in the meantime, it gives me some time to digest and maybe even find some solutions for you. Would that be helpful? Again, that's another style of how you're closing in B2B.

 

[2:59:00] And I would do this with small business owners. I'll get them to think. The good ones will rise to the top. They will think they'll come back to you and they'll communicate more with you.

 

[2:59:30] They'll be like, wow, I never thought of that. That's a great question. I never thought of it from this angle. Automatically you position as an authority. Okay. Next.

 

[3:00:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Plans to Achieve Goals & Overcome Challenges - Adam Role Play"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Transição para próximo set de perguntas com Adam, foco em planos e estratégias para atingir goals e superar challenges

 

[3:00:05] Plans to achieve your goals and overcome challenges. So let's get one more. I'm going to call upon Adam. I know he's dying. He's putting up his hand virtually. He's going, ooh, ooh, ooh, pick me, pick me.

 

[3:00:35] So Adam, jump on. And the reason I'm calling on these three individuals is because they actually do B2B sales with me on a daily basis, right? With Sean, with the team, they actually are engaged in the field.

 

[3:01:05] So I'm bringing them on and we will find other opportunities to bring some of you on as well in future role plays. But for now, Dana, is Adam around? Is he raising his hand? Yes, he is already unmuted. He's already unmuted. Adam, are you there?

 

[3:01:35] Yes, I am. Hello. Can you hear me? Yes, I can, Adam. Okay, so Adam, I

 

[3:02:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Adam Role Play - Multi-threading & Contact Information"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Role play demonstrando coleta de informações de múltiplos stakeholders: nome completo, outros pontos de contato, LinkedIn, research preparation

 

[3:02:05] Happy with Mike. Another point of contact? Yeah. LinkedIn? Yes. Research? Yes. So even though Adam gave me his first name, I made sure to get his last name.

 

[3:02:35] And guess what? Before I get on a call with Mike Silver, I'm going to do my research on Mike Silver. Maybe Adam might get fired next week before I even get on a call with Mike Silver. Now what happens?

 

[3:03:05] I don't have Adam disappears. I don't have his contact. He's moved off to another company. Maybe he quits. Maybe he takes another job position. Maybe he got headhunted and he's gone off to another company.

 

[3:03:35] And this happens very often in the corporate world. B2B sometimes it happens. One moment you're speaking to a point of contact. Next moment you call on the office. Next week you call on the office. They're gone.

 

[3:04:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Corporate B2B Reality - Contact Turnover"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Realidade de B2B corporativo: contatos desaparecem, respostas padronizadas ("no longer works here"), importância de multi-threading para proteção

 

[3:04:10] And they'll give you a canned answer. Oh, such and such a person no longer works here with us. That's it. Oh, do you mind if I have that personal phone number? No. Can't give out that information. Sorry.

 

[3:04:40] So if you're not connected with them with multi-thread individuals, decision makers within the organization, that's going to be very risky for you.

 

[3:05:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "5 Decision Roles in B2B - Custom Framework"

 

NÚMEROS: 5 stakeholders (média), múltiplos roles

 

VISUAL: Framework proprietário com 5 roles nomeados: Creator, Ruler, Explorer, Sage, Hero - cada um com interesse primário e função específica

 

[3:05:15] So these are five that we came up with. We decided to create our own names for them. So B2B sales usually involves on average five stakeholders, including five decision roles.

 

[3:05:45] You have the creator. The person with the primary interest is the return on the investment. They drive the agenda forward and they find or create the budget. So we call them the creator.

 

[3:06:15] You also have the ruler. Now the ruler, their primary interest is allocation and the best use of funds. Many times it could be even the CFO, but not limited to.

 

[3:06:45] Get them involved early to learn the decision criteria. This person sometimes has the ultimate sign off. It could be the CEO. It could be the CRO. It could be the CFO. It could be a partner. It could be a chairman of the board. You need to find out who's the ruler.

 

[3:07:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Explorer - Operational Evaluator"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Definição do Explorer: avalia purchases de perspectiva operacional/pessoal, pergunta "Will this work here?", frequentemente em role de M (manager)

 

[3:07:20] The explorer. Evaluating the purchases from an operational or personal perspective. Asks, will this work here? This is more of the M role sometimes. We've used the term M role. This is the person.

 

[3:07:50] The explorer is normally trying to find out, will this work here? Does it fit into our systems? You're proposing this new SaaS or this new CRM, but will it work in our company? Will it cause us delays and slow down? Will it play well with our other tech stack?

 

[3:08:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Sage - Your Insider"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Definição do Sage: interesse em aprovar projeto ou ajudar pessoalmente, o "insider", favorito de Desmond, fornece informações privilegiadas

 

[3:08:25] Then you have the sage. Primary interest is getting the project approved or helping you succeed personally. Now I personally love the sage. I love working with the sage. The sage is sort of like my insider.

 

[3:08:55] You can see how Adam was becoming my sage. He was becoming my insider a bit. He was helping me, giving me information. That's very, very important.

 

[3:09:25] Simultaneously, Adam could have also been... Keep in mind sometimes too, even though there's five different key decision makers in corporations and companies, it doesn't always have to be five.

 

[3:09:55] Sometimes it could be smaller companies could be two or three. They're wearing multiple hats. Learn to identify. That's why this is a critical screen to take a screenshot of.

 

[3:10:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Multiple Hats Reality & Screenshot Reminder"

 

NÚMEROS: 5 roles (podem ser menos pessoas), 2-3 pessoas (em smaller companies)

 

VISUAL: Realidade: mesma pessoa pode ter múltiplos roles, empresas menores = menos pessoas com mais hats, importância de identificar durante conversas

 

[3:10:30] You may not get it all at once. Maybe even print this out, have it on your desk. As you're having discussions, those of you who are actually actively engaging in B2B sales right now, or those of you who are about to in the very near future, be cognizant of this.

 

[3:11:00] Ask yourself, is the person I'm speaking to, are they a ruler? Do I need to adjust the way I communicate to them? Are they the creator? Are they maybe the hero, the person whose primary interest is influencing the action and direction of the team?

 

[3:11:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Hero - Voice Others Follow"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Definição do Hero: influencia ação e direção do time, voice que outros seguem, diferente do Sage (helper) vs Hero (persuader/influencer)

 

[3:11:35] In swaying the voice that others tend to follow. So there's a difference between the sage. The sage is trying to help me, but the hero is sometimes kind of like the persuader.

 

[3:12:05] The hero sometimes can be your ally, your strongest ally. B2B, the best B2B, the best ultra high ticket closers, they know how to leverage having a hero. Having a hero on the inside of a company.

 

[3:12:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "B2B Corporate World - Revolving Door & Networks"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Conceito de "revolving door" no B2B corporativo: pessoas mudam de empresa, networks se mantêm, importância de manter relacionamentos de longo prazo

 

[3:12:40] And the longer you do B2B, many times the B2B world, especially in the corporate B2B enterprise world, can be somewhat revolving door, can be somewhat incestuous in that sense.

 

[3:13:10] And many times you may know someone who went off to another company, went off to another, and you reach out to that person. And that's why a lot of, you know, I came from Japan and I'm not Japanese, but I lived there for 12 years.

 

[3:13:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Asian Business Culture - Finding Hero & Sage"

 

NÚMEROS: 12 anos (tempo de Desmond no Japão)

 

VISUAL: Exemplo cultural: business entertaining na Ásia (jantares, karaoke), objetivo é encontrar Hero e Sage, obter insider para ajudar na empresa

 

[3:13:45] The way they do business in Asia, there's a lot of the business entertaining, going out for dinners and the karaokes and things like that. Many times what they're doing is they're trying to find the hero and the sage.

 

[3:14:15] They're trying to, you know, get that insider to help them in the company. Okay. Now, not limited to that, you know, there's many ways of doing business, but that was just an example I've experienced. Okay.

 

[3:14:45] Yes. And they are watching you. They're watching to see how you operate, how you behave.

 

[3:15:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "UHTC Secret #4: Leveraging Multi-Thread Conversations"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Introdução ao secret #4: multi-threading, preparação para exemplo real de comunicação multi-stakeholder

 

[3:15:20] So ultra high ticket closing secret number four, it's leveraging the multi-thread conversations. Here's an example of a real life situation here.

 

[3:15:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Real Slack Conversation - Multi-threading Example"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados numericamente

 

VISUAL: Screenshot real de Slack mostrando Gaden Leavitt (CEO/Founder) introduzindo Justin (operations) e Logan (CRO), identificação de múltiplos roles em ação

 

[3:15:55] You can see Gaden Leavitt. Thanks, Desmond. Justin has the keys and can kick off. I know the holiday season can be a little challenging, but we're all committed. This is very recent. Okay.

 

[3:16:25] And you can see over here. So he's the CEO. He's the founder. And then he introduces as well too. You know, thanks for adding Logan. Justin. Justin is kind of the operations person.

 

[3:16:55] So, you know, could you let us know what role Logan plays? And then Logan chimes in. He goes, I'm in charge of the sales department. All training and processes, et cetera, will be coordinated through myself.

 

[3:17:25] I will, you know, I'm what Gaden calls the chief revenue officer. So now I've identified the CRO and I also know sort of the COO is in there. Right. Great to meet you, Logan. Okay. CRO is a very important role.

 

[3:17:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Conversation Continuation - CSM & Scaling Strategy"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Continuação da thread: Raphael (lead CSM promovido), estratégia de proof of concept primeiro depois scale, menção de Justine como referrer

 

[3:18:00] Raphael is our lead CSM. Raphael is no longer CSM. He's definitely graduated to a much higher level than that. He's moved on very well. And he's one of our best leaders too.

 

[3:18:30] I briefed him on this account. There will be work from both sides, but I have told him to make sure our team and on our side does everything to model what you've done already successfully.

 

[3:19:00] Then once we have good proof of concept in place, we can do what we always do best. And that is to scale and bring out more teams while optimizing our processes. Looking forward to working together.

 

[3:19:30] And then Justine, once you have her in there and you're working well, guess what? She became a referrer. She's even reaching out to me now. She says, I have a client that I'm working with and we have some copywriting needs. How do I go about that?

 

[3:20:00] Thanks for thinking of us, Justine. Let me get Rich J., director of service delivery, reach out to you via email. He's presenting at our quarterly meeting right now.

 

[3:20:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Multi-threading Summary - Daily Reality"

 

NÚMEROS: CEO, COO, CRO (múltiplos C-suite em uma empresa)

 

VISUAL: Resumo do exemplo: comunicação simultânea com CRO, COO, CEO dentro de uma empresa, isso acontece diariamente em B2B sales

 

[3:20:35] So you can see this is multi-thread. This is an example of multi-thread. Within one company, I'm communicating to the CRO, to the COO, to the CEO. And this is just a small example. It happens every single day in B2B sales.

 

[3:21:05] Is this helpful for you? All right, moving on.

 

[3:21:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Common B2B Mistake #5: Loving Automation Too Much"

 

NÚMEROS: 5-8 vezes/dia (regra de automação repetida)

 

VISUAL: Erro: amar automação demais, menções de AppSumo, ferramentas, regra de "se faz 5-8x/dia automatizar", mas warning sobre automation backfiring

 

[3:21:40] Common B2B mistake number five. How many of you love automations? You love to find that latest app. You're going to AppSumo. You're trying to find something, some sort of... Okay, don't feel bad. I geek out on this stuff too as well. It's not your fault. We all would love to.

 

[3:22:10] In fact, I've always operated from a basic rule. If I have to do something more than eight times in a day, five to eight times in a day, I need to find an automation.

 

[3:22:40] So if you are looking at what you're doing on a daily basis, if you have to do something more than five to eight times in a day repetitively, you need to find an automation.

 

[3:23:10] On the other hand though, in B2B sales, sometimes automation can actually backfire on you if not used correctly.

 

[3:23:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Automation Tools & Dangers"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Lista de automations: email templates, sequences, Zapier integrations, bot sequences; Dangers: não personalizado, não demonstra relevância, spamming, falta elemento humano

 

[3:23:45] So using email templates, sequences is great. Zapier integrations, bot sequences. On the other hand though, if you don't have things properly copywritten, it's not personalized, you're not demonstrating relevance, you're spamming.

 

[3:24:15] If you don't have the human element, this is where you're gonna run into a brick wall.

 

[3:24:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "UHTC Secret #6: Psychology Meets Technology"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Secret revelado: "Psychology meets technology" - título hybrid da aula retornando, necessidade de balancear ambos para sucesso em B2B

 

[3:24:50] So ultra high ticket closing secret number six. This is where psychology meets technology. What did I say? Psychology meets what? Technology. Type in the chat. Psychology meets technology.

 

[3:25:20] So you have to bring the hybrid of these two together to be successful in B2B. It's not all about tech. Don't be in such a rush to get and go and upload and sign up for enterprise level or business level on all the different softwares.

 

[3:25:50] They can be helpful, but if you lack the psychology, no technology is gonna be helpful for you.

 

[3:26:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "UHTC Secret #7: Start from Empathy"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Secret #7: começar de lugar de empatia forma conexões mais poderosas e significativas, balancear personalização com uso de tecnologia

 

[3:26:25] So let me give you some examples here, okay? Here's a secret number seven. So when you start from a place of empathy, you form more powerful and more meaningful connections.

 

[3:26:55] You balance our personalization and using technology. We do this on a day-to-day basis. Even our B2B team does this on a day-to-day basis. We have templates, we have snippets, but many times, even within our templates, we personalize.

 

[3:27:25] We put things in. We send a message and we let our client know that we're not just copying and pasting some template so that they can feel that we actually genuinely

 

 

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO

 

NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:

Horários de Participação: 2 AM, 3 AM, 4 AM (múltiplos participantes de diferentes time zones)

Origem do HTC: Outubro 2017

Visão de Dan Lok: 10,000 vidas (meta inicial) → 1 bilhão de pessoas (long-term vision)

Idade de Josh Kim: 17 anos (início com Dan Pena) → 21 anos (atual)

Desmond: 33 anos ao entrar em financial services, 18 entrevistas (18/18 aprovações)

Replay: Disponível em 48 horas

 

ESTRUTURAS DE COMPENSAÇÃO B2C vs B2B:

B2C Exemplos: $50-$300 transações pequenas, $40K/ano, $25K base + 20% = $180K

B2B Career Path: Junior SDR $60K OTE → Senior AE $220K OTE

B2B Deal Examples:

Digital Marketing Agency: $10K/mês retainer + 10% rev share = $620K/ano

EB-5 Visa: 4 deals/mês × $500K × 5% = $1.2M/ano; 1 deal/mês = $300K/ano

Danny Lin: $500K deal, 5→6% comissão, 5 deals = $150K

Ralph: $110K em 1 mês

Fortune 500: $9K down + $12K/mês retainer

 

SMB DEFINITIONS:

Até 200 funcionários ou 50 funcionários

Até $50M revenue

Sweet spot: $5-10M revenue

 

STAKEHOLDERS EM B2B:

Média: 5 decision makers

Roles: Creator, Ruler, Explorer, Sage, Hero

C-Suite: CEO, CFO, CMO, COO, CRO

 

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:

 

3 Hábitos de Ultra High Ticket Closers:

Don't make excuses (extreme ownership)

Build credibility (Integrity + Capability = Credibility Value Zone)

Be the trusted advisor (Sun Tzu 506 BC philosophy)

 

Credibility Matrix:

Eixo Vertical: Integrity (trustworthy, reliability, consistency)

Eixo Horizontal: Capability (problem solving, delivery, expert consultant)

Zona Central: Credibility Value Zone

 

Inside vs Outside Sales:

Inside: Prospecting, cold calls/emails, office/remote, smaller recurring deals, sales tools, 6-7 figure income

Outside: Relationship building, networking, travel, deals $35K+, high close rate, longer sales cycle, 80-90% remoto + face-to-face para fechar

 

Multi-threading Strategy:

Coletar múltiplos contatos: nome completo, LinkedIn, outros stakeholders

Proteção contra turnover corporativo

Identificar os 5 roles: Creator, Ruler, Explorer, Sage, Hero

 

Question Framework:

Set 1: Discovery/Business Understanding

Set 2: Achieving Goals/Overcoming Challenges

Set 3: (Fornecido como bônus)

Técnica: Fazer pergunta → Ouvir → Follow-up question mais profunda

"An executive has no time to listen to you, but all day to talk if you ask right questions"

 

Positioning Strategies:

Não precisa ser business owner, mas pode "ter acesso" a recursos

Representar advisory board, billionaires, specialists

"I'm listening, absorbing for you, getting answers"

Feel-Felt-Found technique

 

ESTRUTURA:

 

Mitos vs Verdades B2B:

❌ Precisa degree universitário → ✅ Communication & presentation skills + mentor

❌ Precisa ser mais velho com experiência corporativa → ✅ Size of deal matters, not age

❌ B2B = team sport → ✅ Verdade (SDRs + AEs trabalham juntos)

❌ Precisa fazer B2C antes de B2B → ✅ Pode começar direto em B2B com skills especializadas

 

Erros Comuns & Secrets:

Erro: Apenas phone/email → Secret: Face-to-face às vezes necessário

Erro: Chasing only enterprise deals → Secret: Começar com SMBs

Erro: Não entender business do cliente → Secret: Fazer research, perguntas certas

Erro: Não multi-thread → Secret: Conectar com múltiplos stakeholders

Erro: Automation demais → Secret: Psychology meets technology

 

Terminologia B2B Essencial:

SDR: Sales Development Rep (vs BDR, vs Appointment Setters)

AE: Account Executive (o "closer" em B2B)

OTE: On Target Earnings (não "commission")

OKR: Objective Key Results

CSM: Customer/Client Success Manager

AM: Account Manager

SMB/SME: Small to Medium-sized Business

YPO: Young Presidents Organization

 

Indústrias B2B Promissoras 2020:

Medical & Pharmaceutical (equipamentos, supplies, holistic, regenerative)

(Outras não especificadas em detalhe)

 

DADOS FINANCEIROS:

 

Career Path Progression:

Junior SDR: $40K base + $20K variable = $60K OTE

Senior AE: $220K/ano OTE

Weekends off possível

 

Business Owner Examples:

Digital Agency: $120K retainer + $500K rev share = $620K

EB-5 Broker: $300K (1 deal/mês) a $1.2M (4 deals/mês)

UHTC for Agency: $2K/mês + 2% rev share = $100K+ possível

 

Success Stories:

Danny Lin: $150K (5 EB-5 deals) + convite para abrir agência China

Ralph: $110K em 1 mês ($6K, $9K, $25K, $35-38K, $20K deals)

Fortune 500 client: $9K down + $12K/mês retainer

Tea Time Australia: $74K deal (Zoe credited)

 

Key Metrics:

7 figuras = novo middle class threshold

9 de 10 deals falham quando fora de congruência com 3 hábitos

5-8 repetições/dia = necessidade de automação

80-90% do deal pode ser remoto, 10-20% face-to-face para fechar

 

Assignment Rules:

Replay em 48 horas

Research YPO

Escolher indústria para research profunda

Screenshot slides críticos

Listen to replay antes da próxima aula

 

DURAÇÃO: ~3h 30min (estimado baseado em timestamps) FORMATO: Aula ao vivo com apresentação de slides, role plays, Q&A interativo, exemplos reais, community engagement via Discord
